warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Additives DB (GSFA Online) | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. Users are encouraged to consult the online database and its Preamble for specific provisions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The "Codex General Standard for Food Additives" (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods...

rules.limit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and CCCF established levels
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Codex establishes Maximum Levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities, which are the maximum concentrations legally permitted to protect human health. The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses these levels.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.limit: Follow Code of Practice for prevention and reduction
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: A specific Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) is available for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices CCCF 2017

rules.limit: Conditions of use and approval status must be verified
rules.sourceTitle: Database - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Verification required against the Union list of food additives, which is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.limit: Must comply with food safety criteria and process hygiene criteria
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Verification required as per Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella , Listeria monocytogenes , Enterobacter sakazakii , staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.foodCategory: Raw materials for certain meat and egg products (e.g., poultry, laying hens, breeding hens, meat, eggs)
rules.limit: Must comply with special guarantees for trade
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Special guarantees apply to trade from Sweden, Finland, and Norway. Verification required against Council Decision 95/410/EC, Decision 2004/235/EC, Decision 2003/644/EC, and Regulation (EC) No 1688/2005. Other EU countries can obtain special guarantees if their control program is recognized as equivalent.
rules.evidenceQuote: Special guarantees as regard Salmonella... The current guarantees are laid down in: Council Decision 95/410/EC as regards poultry intended for slaughter Decision 2004/235/EC as regards laying hens Decision 2003/644/EC as regards breeding hens Regulation (EC) No 1688/2005 as regards certain meat and eggs

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.parameter: Class II chemical preservatives (e.g., sorbic acid, benzoic acid)
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are used in the food, the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives must be used to determine permitted levels.
rules.evidenceQuote: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are to be used in the food (e.g. sorbic acid or benzoic acid), use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to calculate the permitted levels that should be used.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA and permitted for use. They must have technological justification and not present health risks. Businesses must check the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool.
rules.evidenceQuote: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food products containing an additive that is not permitted must not be imported or sold.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.limit: Must be within SFA's regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on contaminants in food.
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.limit: Not allowed to exceed regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food products containing contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.parameter: Heavy metals, Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins, Pesticide residues, Other incidental constituents

rules.notes: Businesses must refer to the relevant SFA documents for specific limits on various types of contaminants.
rules.evidenceQuote: There are various limits imposed on different types of contaminants or incidental constituents in food. For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Heavy metals, Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins, Pesticide residues, Other incidental constituents.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: SFA has established a list of Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, available for download from the SFA website.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore. All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.foodCategory: seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Businesses must refer to the relevant SFA documents for specific microbiological standards for ready-to-eat and non-ready-to-eat food.

rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.limit: Must be listed in FDA regulations

rules.limit: Must be listed in FDA regulations or GRAS Notices inventory

rules.notes: These substances are listed in 21 CFR Part 181.
rules.evidenceQuote: Substances approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, known as prior-sanctioned substances (21 CFR Part 181).

rules.notes: Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189).
rules.evidenceQuote: Prohibited substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.foodCategory: Peanuts and peanut products, Pistachio nuts

rules.evidenceQuote: The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels.

rules.limit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content

rules.notes: Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the “gluten-free” claim were established on August 12, 2020.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling

rules.limit: Not required by law; should only be used to address unavoidable cross-contact after good manufacturing practices are followed. Must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: These statements are not a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes

rules.limit: Establishments must implement allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact and undeclared allergens, including written procedures.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Part of the "Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food" rule (21 CFR part 117).
rules.evidenceQuote: The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.parameter: Declaration of Cochineal extract and Carmine
rules.limit: Must be declared on the label
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Identified as allergenic substances derived from insects.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine... must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.limit: Must be declared at least once on the food label
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Declaration can be in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., “lecithin (soy)”) OR immediately after or next to the ingredient list in a “contains” statement (e.g., “Contains wheat, milk, and soy.”). Specific types of tree nuts, fish species, and Crustacean shellfish species must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label in one of two ways.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Must be declared when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
rules.evidenceQuote: they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.

rules.foodCategory: All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Required due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels
